ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Prawn Sinigang; closest defensible match is shrimp soup (dish) FOODON_03311737. Sinigang is a tamarind-based Filipino sour soup with prawns and vegetables.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (shrimp and prawn dishes) used as closest defensible category. No specific Sinigang entry exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Prawn Sinigang or Filipino sour prawn soup.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Prawn Sinigang is one bowl of approximately 350g, comprising broth, prawns, and mixed vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Prawn Sinigang — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Prawn Sinigang (cr_id G00010277). All per_100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record. Saturated fat not reported by HPB for this entry; estimated from USDA FoodData Central shrimp soup analogue (SR Legacy #35196) and documented accordingly.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Prawn Sinigang. GI estimated at 38 (low) based on analogous tamarind-based sour soup with mixed vegetables and seafood. Tamarind has a published GI of approximately 23 (University of Sydney GI Database). The dish's low carbohydrate content (7.92g/100g), high protein (13.24g/100g), and acidic tamarind broth all attenuate glycaemic response. GL per serving calculated as: (38 × 7.92 / 100) × 350g serving = 10.6g. This is a conservative analogous estimate; direct GI testing of Prawn Sinigang has not been published in peer-reviewed literature as of 2026.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 502mg/100g exceeds the 400mg/100g hypertension-safe threshold; a 350g serving delivers approximately 1,757mg sodium, representing 76–88% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000–2,300mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce and avoid drinking large volumes of the broth, as the broth concentrates sodium; retaining potassium-rich vegetables such as kangkong and string beans provides partial offset but is insufficient to neutralise the sodium burden at standard serving sizes.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (7.92g/100g), low sugar (2.96g/100g), high protein (13.24g/100g from prawns), and acidic tamarind broth collectively support a low estimated glycaemic index of approximately 38.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Prawn Sinigang is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without large portions of white rice; the high protein content from prawns supports glycaemic stability, and the tamarind broth's acidity further attenuates postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 502mg/100g poses a cardiovascular risk through blood pressure elevation; however, fat is low (1.22g/100g) and saturated fat is minimal (~0.28g/100g), and prawns provide omega-3 fatty acids and high-quality lean protein.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and request reduced fish sauce to manage sodium intake; the low fat profile and omega-3 contribution from prawns are cardioprotective, but the high sodium content in standard preparations requires active management.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade vegetables (eggplant, tomatoes) are primary ingredients; solanine alkaloids in nightshades may exacerbate joint inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals, compounded by sodium at 502mg/100g which worsens RA-related tissue swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request preparation without eggplant and with reduced tomato, and should request reduced fish sauce to lower sodium; kangkong and string beans provide anti-inflammatory antioxidants and should be retained.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Prawn Sinigang (Sinigang na Hipon) is a classic Filipino sour soup dish widely consumed in the Philippines and among Filipino communities in Singapore. The dish features whole or halved prawns simmered in a tangy tamarind-based broth together with a variety of vegetables including kangkong (water spinach), string beans, eggplant, tomatoes, and radish. The broth is characteristically sour and savoury, with the tartness derived from tamarind pulp or tamarind powder, balanced by fish sauce (patis) which provides saltiness and umami depth. The texture of the dish combines tender, juicy prawns with soft-cooked vegetables in a light, clear, intensely flavoured broth. Nutritionally, Prawn Sinigang is a high-protein, low-fat dish: per 100g it provides 99 kcal, 13.24g protein, 1.22g fat, and 7.92g carbohydrate, with 502mg sodium. The high sodium content is the primary clinical concern, making the dish unsuitable for hypertension patients in standard preparation. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, the dish is well-suited due to its low carbohydrate and sugar content and high protein from prawns. The tamarind broth has a low glycaemic index, further supporting glycaemic stability. Prawn Sinigang is typically served as a main course at lunch or dinner, accompanied by steamed white rice. It is a culturally significant comfort food in Filipino cuisine, representing the sinigang family of sour soups which may also be prepared with pork, beef, or fish.
common_user_queries: Is Sinigang na Hipon good for diabetics?
How much sodium is in Prawn Sinigang?
Can hypertension patients eat Prawn Sinigang?
What is the glycemic index of Sinigang?
What vegetables are in Sinigang na Hipon?
How many calories in a bowl of Prawn Sinigang?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for Prawn Sinigang (cr_id G00010277) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy shrimp soup analogue and documented. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database tamarind and seafood soup data, clearly documented as estimated.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.28g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy shrimp soup entry (closest approved-source analogue) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this dish. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data for Prawn Sinigang are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this specific dish as of 2026. 3. GI of 38 is an analogous estimate; no direct peer-reviewed GI measurement for Prawn Sinigang has been published as of 2026. The estimate is based on tamarind GI (~23, University of Sydney GI Database) and the dish's macronutrient profile (high protein, low carbohydrate, acidic broth). 4. Prawn Sinigang is classified as a Filipino dish consumed in Singapore; multilingual names provided in Chinese (Simplified and Traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per Singaporean multilingual naming rules. 5. Halal status assessed as likely halal (prawns and vegetables with fish sauce); kosher status not confirmed. 6. Nightshade vegetables (eggplant, tomatoes) are primary ingredients — relevant for rheumatoid arthritis caution.
